text: Eli Pariser coined the term 'filter bubble' in 2011 to describe the invisible algorithmic curation that seals each user inside a personalized information world. Unlike a newspaper, which exposes readers to editors' choices across the political and cultural spectrum, a filter bubble shows you only what your past behavior predicts you will click. Pariser demonstrated this by asking friends with different political leanings to Google the same term — they received dramatically different results. The danger is not mere inconvenience; it is epistemic. When your information environment is curated to confirm what you already believe, your model of reality quietly diverges from your neighbors'.
question: What danger does Pariser identify as the primary consequence of filter bubbles?

options.text: Users spend more time online than they intend to
Search engines become slower due to personalization overhead
Each person's model of reality quietly diverges from others'
Advertisers gain too much influence over editorial decisions
